candidates.diagnosis: Stanford Type A aortic dissection with malperfusion
supporting_evidence: UNIFYING DIAGNOSIS: simultaneous transient neurological deficit (carotid malperfusion) AND melena (mesenteric malperfusion) — two-territory involvement strongly suggests a proximal aortic process
Bicuspid aortic valve confers 9-fold increased risk of aortic dissection independent of aortic dimensions
Ascending aortic root dilated to 4.2 cm on last imaging 3 years ago — with a growth rate of 0.08 cm/year from prior echo, current root likely 4.4-4.5 cm (approaching surgical threshold)
LAPSED SURVEILLANCE: follow-up echo due February 2024, never completed — 2+ years of unmonitored aortic root growth
Uncontrolled hypertension for 25 years (recent readings 148-164 systolic despite amlodipine) — chronic aortic wall shear stress
Former 40-pack-year smoker — accelerates medial degeneration and cystic medial necrosis in bicuspid valve aortopathy

The absence of classic tearing chest pain does NOT exclude dissection — painless dissection occurs in 5-15% of cases, particularly in elderly patients and those with neurological malperfusion as the dominant presentation

candidates.diagnosis: TIA secondary to carotid dissection extension
supporting_evidence: If Type A dissection is present, the dissection flap frequently extends into the brachiocephalic trunk and right carotid artery
Carotid malperfusion causes transient neurological deficits indistinguishable from atherothrombotic TIA
Resolution of symptoms may represent intermittent flap obstruction with restored flow
This is not a separate diagnosis from aortic dissection but rather a manifestation of it

candidates.diagnosis: Upper GI bleeding (aspirin-related) concurrent with separate vascular event
supporting_evidence: Aspirin 75 mg daily is a known cause of gastric erosion and peptic ulceration
Melena is consistent with upper GI source above the ligament of Treitz
If two separate pathologies: coincidental aspirin-related GI bleed + atherothrombotic TIA
Less likely than a unifying diagnosis given simultaneous onset of both symptom complexes
Acute stroke can trigger stress gastritis via sympathetic activation
However, patient's neurological symptoms were transient and fully resolved
Cushing ulcer mechanism typically requires severe neurological injury
Less likely given the mild and transient nature of the neurological episode
Known ascending aortic dilation (4.2 cm, likely larger now after 3 years without surveillance)
Contained aortic rupture can present insidiously without dramatic haemodynamic collapse
Aortoenteric fistula from aneurysm erosion into adjacent bowel could explain melena
BP 152/88 appears stable but may be pseudo-normal if compensating for blood loss

reasoning: This case exemplifies a fundamental clinical principle: when a patient presents with symptoms in two separate organ systems simultaneously, always seek a UNIFYING DIAGNOSIS before accepting coincidence. A 72-year-old with a bicuspid aortic valve, dilated aortic root (4.2 cm, lapsed surveillance), 25 years of poorly controlled hypertension, and a 40-pack-year smoking history has an extraordinarily high pre-test probability for acute aortic pathology. The simultaneous appearance of transient neurological symptoms (consistent with carotid malperfusion) and melena (consistent with mesenteric malperfusion) maps perfectly onto Stanford Type A dissection with multi-territory involvement. The critical next step is BILATERAL ARM BP measurement — a differential >20 mmHg strongly supports dissection — followed by emergent CTA aorta. The absence of chest pain is a well-documented pitfall in aortic dissection diagnosis and should NOT provide false reassurance.
